coredata.dc:title: Defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode modified graphene quantum dots as a novel surface-renewable electrode: The application to determination of zolpidem
coredata.dc:creator: Dehgan-Reyhan, S.
Najafi, M.
coredata.dc:description: A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor was developed for detection and determination of zolpidem (Zlp) based on graphene quantum dots modified defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode (DMCCE-GQDs). The cyclic voltammetry (CV), choronoamperometry (CA), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques were used for electrochemical studies and drug determination. The electrocatalytic currents increased with the Zlp concentration and exhibit two linear dynamic ranges with a detection limit of 0.061μM Zlp (S/N=3). The linear calibration ranges were obtained between 0.1 and 1 and 1–10μM Zlp using the DPV method. The diffusion coefficient (D=4.2×10−5 cm2 s−1) and the heterogeneous rate constant, (k=7.53×102 cms−1) for Zlp were determined. Finally, the modified electrode was successfully applied to determination of Zlp in tablet dosage forms with a mean recovery of 97.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Defective mesoporous carbon
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor
Zolpidem
Ceramic electrode
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of defective mesoporous carbon (DMC) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrode
Real sample analysis and interference study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of (curve a) bare DMCCE and (curve b) DMCCE-GQDs in 1:1 ratio of 5mM [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 in 0.1M KCl. (B) CVs of the CCE (curve a), DMCCE (curve b), DMCCE-GQDs (curve c) were recorded in the presence of 0.5mM Zlp in a solution of 0.5mM KNO3 (pH7.2, phosphate buffer) at scan rate 100mVs−1.
Voltammograms of the DMCCE-GQDs in the presence of 0.5mM Zlp in different pH values over the range of 5–9. Inset: influence of pH on the peak current.

Cyclic voltammograms of DMCCE-GQDs in 0.1M phosphate buffer (pH7.2), different potential scan rates (10, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350mVs−1) in the presence of 0.5mM ZLP. Insets: (A) variations of Ip versus square root of the scan rate (B) variation of Ip/v1/2) versus potential scan rate. (C) The Tafel plot derived from the cyclic voltammograms at low scan rate.

A) Chronoamperograms obtained at DMCCE-GQDs in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH7.2) for different concentration (0.125–0.5mM) of Zlp by setting potential step at 900mV. Insets: (a) Plots of I vs. t−1/2/s−1/2 obtained from chronoamprograms (b) plot of the slope of the straight lines against the Zlp concentration. (B) Dependence of Icat/Il on t1/2/s1/2 derived from the data of chronoamperograms.

A) DPVs of the DMCCE-GQDs in the solution containing Zlp at different concentrations: 0, 0.1, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 4, 6, 8, 10μM, respectively. B) Plots of electrocatalytic peak current as a function of Zlp concentration in the range of 0.1–10μM.
Figures of merit of suggested electrode for the determination of ZLP.

row.entry: 0.7975 for first range and 5.785 for second range

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of some figures of merit of suggested electrodes for the determination of ZLP.
Result of determination and recovery of zolpidem in its formulations.

head.ce:title: Defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode modified graphene quantum dots as a novel surface-renewable electrode: The application to determination of zolpidem

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Imam Hossein University, Tehran 16597, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sensitive electrochemical sensor was developed for detection and determination of zolpidem (Zlp) based on graphene quantum dots modified defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode (DMCCE-GQDs). The cyclic voltammetry (CV), choronoamperometry (CA), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques were used for electrochemical studies and drug determination. The electrocatalytic currents increased with the Zlp concentration and exhibit two linear dynamic ranges with a detection limit of 0.061μM Zlp (S/N=3). The linear calibration ranges were obtained between 0.1 and 1 and 1–10μM Zlp using the DPV method. The diffusion coefficient (D=4.2×10−5 cm2 s−1) and the heterogeneous rate constant, (k=7.53×102 cms−1) for Zlp were determined. Finally, the modified electrode was successfully applied to determination of Zlp in tablet dosage forms with a mean recovery of 97.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Zolpidem (Fig. 1 ) (N,N,6-trimethyl-2-(4-methylphenyl) imidazo (1,2-a) pyridine-3-acetamide), is a non-benzodiazepine sedative and hypnotic/psychoactive medicine [1,2]. It is also used for short-term treatment of sleep problems that is widely prescribed for adults. After oral administration, Zlp is rapidly absorbed and has a favorable bioavailability of about 70% [3,4]. The Zlp is categorized as rapid onset and short half-life (between 1.4 and 4.5h). Unlike benzodiazepines, it is an effective hypnotic medicine with weak antifungal and muscle relaxant properties [5,6]. This drug is converted into three metabolites by oxidation and hydroxylation in the liver [7,8]. Gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [9], spectrophotometry [10–12], capillary electrophoresis [13], potentiometry [14], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15,16,18], LC/MS/MS [19], and radioimmunoassay [20] have been utilized for Zlp determination. Although, previous methods exhibit good accuracy and precision, most of them are often tedious, time-consuming and require expensive equipments. Moreover, their sample preparations are vast. Therefore, development of a simple and fast analytical method is essential for detection of drugs in pharmaceutical ingredients or biological samples.

Electrochemical methods have advantages such as inexpensive production, ease of use and rapid response. They are favorable for detection and determination of different analytes. Reviewing the literature indicates that only a few papers were reported on the use of electrochemical sensors in detection and determination of Zlp [21,22]. In these reports, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [21] and pencil graphite electrode (PGE) [22] were used to determine zolpidem without any modification. The use of modified electrodes can improve the sensitivity and detection limit of electrochemical methods for determination of zolpidem. Therefore, there are many opportunities for using the new electrochemical sensor in Zlp determination.

Mesoporous carbon materials (MCMs) are a type of carbon nanostructures which provide a medium for redox processes in electrochemical devices. These materials have good conductivity, chemical inertness and vast electrochemical stability window [23,24]. The high specific area of MCMs attracts considerable interest in the manufacture of electrochemical sensors and biosensors.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered as a 0D material with specifications derived from both QDs and graphene [25]. Due to the new characteristics such as chemical stability, low toxicity and stable photoluminescence, GQDs are regarded as advanced materials for various applications [26,27]. This paper deals with the use of GQDs for modification of defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode and development of a voltammetric procedure for determination of Zlp in pharmaceutical formulations. The construction of the ceramic electrode is much cheaper and simpler than the GCE. Also, the mechanical strength and adsorption of this electrode is higher than the PGE electrode.

Methyltrimethoxysilane (MTMOS), KNO3, NaOH, H2SO4, HCl, K3[Fe(CN)6], Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and other reagents were analytical grade and purchased from Merck or Aldrich. Graphite powder (extra pure; particle size<50m) was produced by Merck. Zolpidem tartrate was obtained from OSVE pharmaceutical Co. (Tehran, Iran). Zolpirest® tablets (contain 5 and 10mg zolpidem tartrate) were purchased from local pharmacies.

Stock solution of Zlp (100μM) in doubly distilled water was prepared and kept in the refrigerator at +4°C. The pH of cell solution was adjusted to required pH value using dilute H2SO4 or NaOH solutions in the range of 4.0 to 9.0. Then phosphate buffer (0.1M pH=7.2) was used for measurements as optimized pH.

The electrochemical studies were carried out with an μAutolab potentiostat/galvanostat type III/FRA2 equipped with NOVA software. A three-electrode system consisted of a modified electrode (DMCCE-GQDs), a platinum rod as an auxiliary electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode (Azar Electrode Co, Iran). The Minitab16 (Minitab Inc., USA) software was used for experimental design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of defective mesoporous carbon (DMC) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: DMC was synthesized and characterized by the previously described report [23]. Briefly, a homogenous mixture containing of 2g of sucrose, 5.0g of KNO3, 4g of nanosillica and 100mL of deionized water was prepared and then dried at 80°C for 10h. The product was heated at 140°C for 3h and then carbonized at 800°C for 1h under argon gas. Finally, the carbon powder was prepared by etching the silica template in 1M solution of NaOH via reflux at 80°C.

GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal technique [28]. Initially, graphene oxide was obtained by chemical oxidization of graphite powder based on the modified Hummers method [29]. A tube furnace at temperature of 200–300°C and heating rate of 5°Cmin1 was applied to deoxidation of graphene oxide for 2h under the nitrogen atmosphere. The gained graphene sheets (0.05g) were oxidized in mixture of 10mL concentrated H2SO4 and 30mL HNO3 for 15–20h under ultrasonication (500W, 40kHz). Oxidized graphene sheets were purified by microporous membranes and redispersed in deionized water. Afterwards the mixture was heated in an autoclave at 200°C for 10h. Finally, the black suspension was filtered through microporous membrane and a brown filtrate solution (GCDs) was obtained.

The bare CCE was prepared based on a procedure developed by Lev and co-workers [30]. The original manufacture method was as follows: A blend of 0.75mL CH3OH, 0.5mL MTMOS and 0.05mL HCl (12M) was prepared and stirred for 10min until a homogeneous milky gel solution resulted. Then, 1.4g graphite powder was added to the mixture and stirred for 5min to form a uniform paste. Next, the produced mixture was packed into a glass tube (with 5cm length and 3mm inner diameter) and dried at room temperature for 24h. The DMCCE was constructed like the above-mentioned procedure except for addition of desired amount of DMC (about 3% w/w) to graphite powder. The modified electrodes (DMCCE-GQDs) were made by adding 20μL of GQD solutions (0.05, 0.1 and 0.2M) on the surface of the DMCCE and dried at room temperature. Afterwards 20μL of chitosan solution (1.0% w/v chitosan in 1.0% (v/v) acetic acid solution) was poured on the surface of the electrode and dried at room temperature. In order to activate the surface of the electrode, a potential of 1.2V for 100s was applied to DMCCE-GQDs.

In order to reduce the number of experiments and obtain the optimal parameters in the intensity of electrooxidation current, Taguchi method was used. Having applied Taguchi method, a small number of experiments are required to cover the entire parameter influence. A particular design of the orthogonal arrays [OA] L9 was utilized for the three controllable factors including, pH, and concentration of GQDs. The procedure is presented in the Electronic Supporting Material in detail.

For the optimization of differential pulse conditions, the amplitude of potential, pulse width and pulse period were examined varying one of them and maintaining the others constant. The studied variable ranges were 10–100mV for the amplitude of potential, 5–50ms for the pulse width and scan rate from 5.0 to 100mVs−1. The optimized voltammetric parameters were pulse amplitude: 50mV, pulse width: 25.0ms and scan rate: 50mVs−1.

Twenty five tablets, each containing 5 or 10mg/tablet of Zlp, were accurately weighed and finely powdered. A weighed portion of the powder content equivalent to 1mM of Zlp was transferred to a 100mL beaker and 40mL distilled water was added. The mixture was stirred magnetically for 5min, then filtered into a 100mL volumetric flask and completed to the mark with distilled water. Appropriate solutions were prepared by taking suitable aliquots of Zlp solution and diluting with phosphate buffer (0.1M pH=7.2). To study the accuracy of the proposed methods, recovery experiments were carried out using the standard addition method.

The structural features of the synthesized DMC were studied in detail in the previous report [23]. The SEM micrograph of bare carbon ceramic electrode (CCE), (Fig. 2A) obviously indicates smooth and uniform surface, whereas the high surface area and 3D layered pore structure of the DMCCE is observed in Fig. 2B.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is one of the useful electrochemical techniques developed by Warburg that provides detailed data on impedance variations of electrochemical systems in electrode interface. The EIS spectrum of an electrode is usually examined in three main sections with three complex processes. (i) The first part is the region of high frequencies (>1kHz), which is related to the equivalent series resistance. (ii) The second part is related to the medium and low frequency region, which shows the effect of capacitance in this region. (iii) The third region is related to low frequencies (<5Hz) and attributed to the semi-infinite diffusion of ion in the electrode/electrolyte interface which is called the Warburg diffusion region.

Fig. 3A shows the Nyquist plots of the various electrodes in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 containing 0.1M KNO3 as the supporting electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 3A curve a, the DMCCE impedance spectrum indicates a nearly straight line at all frequencies corresponding to the diffusion process. The low Rct on the bare DMCCE exhibits a fast electron transfer rate between the DMCCE and the [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 . On the other hand, a semicircular diameter is observed in the DMCCE-GQDs (Fig. 3A, curve b). This suggests that GQDs on the surface of DMCCE decrease the rate of electron transfer between the [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 and the electrode surface compared with DMCCE. This is due to the presence of negatively charged groups such as the COOH group at the GQD surface, which prevent [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4 from penetrating the electrode/solution interference [31,32]. However, according to the Fig. 3A curve b, it is also clear that the resistance of the modified electrode with quantum dots is decreased compared with the bare DMCCE. This indicates an increase in the diffusion process on the surface of the modified electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Although GQDs are not good conductive materials, they have features such as high surface area, hydrophilic edges, good compatibility with other compounds, low toxicity and environment- friendly properties. Therefore, GQDs can be considered as an excellent modifier in electrochemical sensors. Fig. 3B shows a typical cyclic voltammograms obtained with 0.5mM Zlp at pH7.2 and scan rate of 100mVs−1, using CCE, DMCCE and DMCCE-GQDs. Obviously, the oxidation peak current of Zlp at DMCCE-GQDs is increased dramatically compared with the CCE and DMCCE. According to the Fig. 3B, oxidation potentials of Zlp (Ep) for DMCCE and DMCCE-GQDs are approximately +0.95V and 0.97V respectively. Nevertheless, higher oxidation peak current is observed at DMCCE-GQDs (66μA) compared with DMCCE (15μA) for 0.5mM Zlp. This rise in the peak current occurs as a result of increase in the electron transfer rate in the presence of GQDs.

The electrochemical behavior of Zlp shows a strong dependence on pH (Fig. 4 ). As seen, with the increase of pH, the oxidation peak shifted toward negative values and the peak intensity increased considerably at pH values around 7.0. Thus the phosphate buffer solution with pH7.2 is applied as the supporting electrolyte in all electrochemical procedure.

Furthermore, a plot of current function (Ip/v1/2) versus potential scan rate (Fig. 5 inset B) illustrates a usual shape of an EC catalytic process. In order to investigate the electrooxidation mechanism of Zlp on the modified electrode, some kinetic parameters such as the electron transfer coefficient (α) and electrons involved in the rate determination step of Zlp electrochemical reaction (nα) have been calculated.

The inset (C) of Fig. 5 depicts a tafel diagram that is obtained at a low scan rate (10mVs−1) and the equation is got as Epa (V)=0.181 log I (I: μA)+0.7122 (R2 =0.9966). For an irreversible reaction, the tafel slope is calculated according to the Eq. (1). The slope of 0.181V/decade is obtained from tafel plot, which indicates the involvement of one electron in the rate determination step. Therefore, a transfer coefficient (α) is obtained 0.67. If two electrons are assumed to be in the rate determination step, an α-value should be accepted 0.84, which is not a common value, since for most electrodes, α is in the range of 0.2 to 0.7 [33]:
(1)
Slope = 0.059 /
1 − α
n α

Eq. (2) is used for the calculation of total n in the totally irreversible redox process [33]:
(2)
I p
= 3.01 ×
10 5
n
1 − α
n α
1 / 2
A
C ZLP
D
1 / 2
υ
1 / 2

In Eq. (2), n is the number of electrons participating in the electrochemical reaction, A is the electrode surface area (cm2), C is the concentration of Zlp in solution (molcm−3), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1) of Zlp (that obtain 4.2×10−5 cm2 s−1 from chronoamperometric study in the next section) and υ is the scan rate (mVs−1). According to the plot of the IP versus the square root of the scanning rate (Fig. 5A), the value of total n can be calculated if other variables are known. Taking the slope of Fig. 5A (3.57μA/(mVs−1)1/2) into consideration, the value of n is estimated 1.7, which is close to 2. Given the obtained kinetic data, in probable oxidation mechanism of the Zlp, two protons and two electrons might be included, which agrees with the previous report [22].

The catalytic oxidation of Zlp by DMCCE-GQDs is studied by choronoamperometry. Fig. 6A illustrates the chronoamperograms obtained at the potential step of 900mV. These chronoamperograms have been used for the determination of diffusion coefficient of Zlp. For any electroactive material with a diffusion coefficient of D (cm2 s−1), the electrochemical reaction current (at a mass transport limited rate) is defined by the Cottrell equation [33]:
(3)
I =
nFAD
1 / 2
C b
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2
where A represents the surface area of the electrode (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1) and C is the bulk concentration (molcm−3). Under diffusion control process, a plot of I versus t−1/2 will be linear (Fig. 6A inset a), and the value of D can be obtained from the slope of the plot. Inset (b) in Fig. 6 shows the fitted experimental plots for different concentrations of Zlp. The slopes of the resulting straight lines were plotted versus the Zlp concentration (Fig. 6A inset b) and the mean value of D is found to be 4.2×10−5 cm2 s−1. The Galus method is used to evaluate the catalytic rate constant (Eq. (4)), k/M−1 s−1, for the reaction between Zlp and the DMCCE-GQDs [34]:
(4)
I C
/
I L
=
γ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
erf
γ
1 / 2
+ exp
−
γ
1 / 2
/
γ
1 / 2
where IC is the catalytic current of Zlp at the DMCCE-GQDs, IL is the limited current in the absence of Zlp and γ=kCbt (Cb is the bulk concentration of Zlp) is the argument of error function. When γ exceeds 2, the error function approximates 1 and the Eq. (4) can be shortened to Eq. (5), where t (s) is the elapsed time.
(5)
I C
/
I L
=
π
1 / 2
γ
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
k
C b
t
1 / 2

The above equation can be used to gain the rate constant k of the catalytic process. Based on the slope of the IC/IL versus t1/2 plot (Fig. 6B); k can be gained for a given Zlp concentration. Such plots achieved from the chronoamperograms in Fig. 6A are illustrated in Fig. 6B. From the values of the slopes, the average value of k is found to be 7.53×102 M−1 s−1. The value of k describes a striking feature of the Zlp catalytic oxidation at the DMCCE-GQDs surface.

Since the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) has higher current, better resolution and a better sensitivity than cyclic voltammetry, it was applied to determine Zlp. Fig. 7A shows the differential pulse voltammograms obtained in different concentrations of Zlp at the DMCCE-GQDs. The dependence of the peak current on the Zlp concentration is shown in Fig. 7B. As can be seen, the peak currents' intensities increase as the Zlp concentration rises. Fig. 7B obviously show that the plot of peak currents versus the Zlp concentration consists of two linear ranges with different slopes. The linear equations are Ipa (μA)=5.7852C (μmolL−1)+0.1366 (0.1–1μmolL−1, R =0.9919) and Ipa (μA)=0.7975C (μmolL−1)+4.9343 (1–10μmolL−1, R =0.9877). A summary of figures of merit in determination of Zlp by DMCCE-GQDs is given in Table 1 .

The decrease in slope (sensitivity) in the second linear range (Fig. 7B), is probably due to kinetic limitation. From the analysis of this data, the detection limit of Zlp is calculated 0.06μM (S/N=3). This value is comparable with values reported by other similar researches (Table 2 ).

The precision of the method was evaluated by repeating three experiments on the same day (within-day precision) and over 7days (day-to-day precision). The relative standard deviation values from intra-day and inter-day analysis were found to be (0.26–3.7%) and (1.4–4.1%) at concentration of 4×10−6 M. The stability study of the DMCCE-GQDs was carried out in 0.5M KNO3 solution (pH7.2, 0.1M PBS). When the electrode potential was swept frequently in the range of 0.4 to 1.2V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 for 50cycles, the peak intensity of the cyclic voltammograms didn't indicate considerable drop. Moreover, the long term stability of the DMCCE–GQDs was obtained over a month, when they were stored at the refrigerator temperature in a laboratory.

To investigate the reproducibility of the DMCCE–GQDs, electrochemical oxidation of 0.5mM Zlp at the same conditions were performed by using 3 different DMCCE–GQDs. The electrode responses declined by almost 6%, indicating the proper stability of DMCCE–GQDs. According to this study, the fabricated modified electrodes showed current responses (RSD=4%, n =3) proving that the manufacture process of electrodes is suitably reproducible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and interference study
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of Zlp in its powdered tablets was performed by the standard addition method. The data given in Table 3 clearly indicates a good recovery for drug determinations as well as the drug declared amounts in the used drug preparations. This is indicative of noninterference of the other ingredients and the excipients which are present in the formulations.

Zolpidem is extensively metabolized by human cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes to inactive metabolites and <1% of a dose is excreted in urine as unchanged drug [19]. Nevertheless, assuming that the amount of drug in the urine is in the range of detection limit of the electrochemical system, the interference of different species was investigated. The results indicated that the developed electrochemical sensor has an appropriate selectivity for Zlp determination (Fig. S2 in the Electronic Supporting Material).

This study describes a simple procedure for construction of modified defective mesoporous carbon ceramic electrode with graphene quantum dots (DMCCE-GQDs). Constructed ceramic sensor has a good chemical and mechanical stability and is considered as a reliable and appropriate sensor in comparison with other similar sensors available in scientific resources. The modified electrode can be used for electrochemical determination of other electroactive drugs and chemical compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by Imam Hossein University, Tehran, Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.10.061.
